title: One Bed, Two Lives
desc: After a mass-casualty event, one monitored ICU bed remains. Two patients need it now: a young teacher with multisystem trauma and an elderly official with septic shock.

patient.name: Crisis cohort (2 patients)
patient.condition: Nadia 26 polytrauma vs Mr. Benali 71 septic shock — one monitored ICU bed

law.text: When resources are scarce, decisions must follow transparent medical criteria — prognosis, urgency, and likelihood of benefit — not wealth, status, or age alone.

complications.title: Ministry calls
complications.premise: An official asks you to reserve capacity.
complications.liveLine: 📞 "Doctor, Director Amrani must have priority — you understand what this means for the hospital budget."

complications.title: Journalist filming ER
complications.premise: A reporter asks who received the "last bed."
complications.liveLine: "If you chose the young teacher over a ministry advisor, say it on camera — people deserve transparency."

complications.title: Families screaming
complications.premise: Both families beg you to decide in their favor.
complications.liveLine: Two groups press against the desk — "Choose him!" / "She has three children!" Security is thin.

complications.title: Distant ICU maybe
complications.premise: Another city might accept one patient in four hours.
complications.liveLine: Transfer coordinator: "Helicopter is weather-grounded — road team can move one stabilised patient after fluids, not both."

steps.msg: Charge nurse: "We have one bed. Nadia, 26, polytrauma — GCS improving but needs monitoring. Mr. Benali, 71, sepsis — pressors maxed on the floor. Both will deteriorate without ICU in the hour."
steps.q: What is your first ethical move?
steps.hint: Avoid arbitrary decisions; use transparent criteria:

choices.label: Convene rapid multi-professional triage
choices.reaction: The team can breathe — we're not improvising alone.

choices.label: You choose alone by instinct
choices.reaction: Whispers start — "He picked the younger one because cameras were rolling."

choices.label: Strict first-arrival rule
choices.reaction: Medically it might be wrong — but at least it's a rule.

choices.label: Ask hospital director to decide
choices.reaction: Clinicians feel abandoned — families smell politics.

steps.msg: Scores suggest similar short-term mortality without ICU, but Nadia may gain more life-years if she survives the night; Mr. Benali might respond faster to ICU pressors — unclear. A donor offers to pay for private transport abroad for "whoever you choose."
steps.q: How do you handle the offer and the tie?
steps.hint: Justice forbids selling priority; medical benefit should dominate:

choices.label: Decline payment-linked priority
choices.reaction: Staff nods — the rule stayed clean.

choices.label: Let donor pick who gets transport
choices.reaction: Crowd outrage: "Ethics sold to the highest bidder."

choices.label: Genuine lottery between clinically equal options

steps.msg: You commit the bed using documented prognosis criteria. The excluded patient's family threatens litigation and accuses discrimination against elders.
steps.q: How do you respond after allocation?
steps.hint: Transparency and continued duty of care matter:

choices.label: Thorough documented rationale + family debrief
choices.reaction: They grieve, but the process had integrity.

choices.label: Maximize floor care for the non-admitted
choices.reaction: We did everything possible without ICU.

debrief.summary: Rationing in crises requires pre-agreed medical criteria, multi-person deliberation when possible, and rejection of wealth or politics as tie-breakers. After the decision, honesty and maximal non-ICU care honor the patient who did not receive the scarce resource.
points: Justice is procedural — opaque "gut" choices erode trust in all future triage.
Age alone must not determine worth; life-years may be one factor among many, not a rigid rule.
Payment must never purchase survival priority in public emergencies.
Document rationale to support staff morally and legally under scrutiny.
